ontology.foodon_gap_note: No dedicated FoodOn URI for sweet potato fries; closest match is the generic french fries food product class. Sweet potato fries are a sweet-potato-based variant of deep-fried potato strips.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers fried sweet potato products; used as the closest defensible match for deep-fried sweet potato strip preparations.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for sweet potato fries as a distinct food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of sweet potato fries is approximately 100 g, equivalent to a small side-order portion as commonly served in cafes or fast-food outlets.

nutrient_source.title: Sweet Potato Fries — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for sweet potato fries (deep-fried sweet potato strips), CR ID F14060016. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 61 (medium) for deep-fried sweet potato fries is estimated based on published GI values for sweet potato preparations in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Boiled sweet potato GI ranges 44–94 depending on variety and cooking method; deep-frying raises the effective GI relative to boiling. A GI of 61 is a defensible mid-range estimate for deep-fried sweet potato strips. Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (61 × 36.2) / 100 = 22.1, classified as high GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 362 mg/100 g combined with high fat from deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit sweet potato fries due to sodium content of 362 mg/100 g; request baked sweet potato with minimal added salt as a lower-sodium alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI of 61 combined with high carbohydrate (36.2 g/100 g), high sugar (10 g/100 g), and high glycemic load of 22.1 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid deep-fried sweet potato fries due to the high glycemic load of 22.1 and elevated sugar content; if sweet potato is desired, choose boiled or baked preparations with a substantially lower GI and pair with protein to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 19.82 g/100 g from deep-frying, including saturated fat ~2.5 g and trace trans fat 0.1 g/100 g, combined with high sodium 362 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid deep-fried sweet potato fries due to the high total fat content from frying, presence of trans fat at 0.1 g/100 g which is pro-atherogenic, and borderline-high sodium; baked sweet potato is a heart-healthier alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; trace trans fat 0.1 g/100 g is pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried sweet potato fries as deep-frying generates AGEs that exacerbate joint inflammation; baked sweet potato retains anti-inflammatory beta-carotene and vitamin C without the AGE burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sweet potato fries are deep-fried strips of sweet potato (Ipomoea batatas) cooked in vegetable oil until golden and crisp on the outside with a soft, slightly sweet interior. They are a popular snack and side dish served in cafes, fast-food outlets, and restaurants internationally, including Singapore. The deep-frying process significantly increases the fat content to approximately 19.82 g per 100 g and raises the glycemic index relative to boiled or baked preparations. Each 100 g serving provides 332 kcal, 36.2 g carbohydrate, 10 g sugar, 3.5 g dietary fibre, and 362 mg sodium. The glycemic load per 100 g serving is 22.1, classified as high, making this food unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The presence of trace trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) from frying oil and the formation of advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) during deep-frying also make this food unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. Sweet potato itself is a nutritious vegetable rich in beta-carotene, potassium (~300 mg/100 g), calcium (36 mg/100 g), and iron (0.58 mg/100 g); however, the deep-frying preparation method substantially diminishes its health benefits. Baked or boiled sweet potato is recommended as a healthier alternative for all clinical populations.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics eat sweet potato fries?
How many calories in sweet potato fries?
Is sweet potato fries good for high blood pressure?
Sweet potato fries vs regular fries nutrition
Can I eat sweet potato fries if I have heart disease?
Are sweet potato fries suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F14060016) at confidence 0.95. GI value of 61 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database published values for sweet potato preparations, which is the approved GI source. All mandatory fields are complete and supported.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for deep-fried sweet potato fries not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database entry F14060016. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source for this preparation. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient detail beyond calcium and iron not reported in the HPB Foodo source for this specific fried preparation. potassium_mg value of 300 mg/100 g is an estimate based on USDA FoodData Central data for fried sweet potato (NDB 11510 analogue) as HPB does not report potassium for this entry. trans_fat_g of 0.1 g/100 g is carried forward from the input health suitability assessment as a plausible trace value for deep-fried products; not explicitly reported in HPB source. saturated_fat_g of 2.5 g/100 g estimated from USDA FoodData Central fried sweet potato analogue as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. cholesterol_mg confirmed 0.0 as sweet potato is a plant-based food with no cholesterol. vitamin_b12_mcg confirmed 0.0 as this is a plant-based food. vitamin_d_mcg confirmed 0.0 as sweet potato contains negligible vitamin D. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database published sweet potato GI range; deep-frying raises GI relative to boiling. GL per serving calculated as (61 × 36.2) / 100 = 22.1.
